# Pauli Group P_1 = ⟨σ_i⟩ Has Order 16 on Per-Site Cl(3) Hilbert

**Date:** 2026-05-02
**Type:** positive_theorem
**Claim scope:** the multiplicative group generated by the Pauli matrices
σ_1, σ_2, σ_3 on the framework's per-site Hilbert H_x ≅ C² (cited per-site
uniqueness) has exactly **|P_1| = 16** elements: P_1 = {±I, ±i I, ±σ_a, ±i σ_a}
for a = 1, 2, 3. Center Z(P_1) = {±I, ±i I} of order 4; quotient
P_1 / Z(P_1) ≅ Z_2 × Z_2 (Klein four-group). Every element has order
dividing 4. This is the framework's per-site **single-qubit Pauli group**,
the fundamental finite group structure of Cl(3) at the operator level.
**Status authority:** independent audit lane only. This source note does
not set or predict an audit outcome; later status is generated by the
audit pipeline after independent review.
**Loop:** `positive-only-r8-20260502`
**Cycle:** 3 (Block 3)
**Branch:** `physics-loop/positive-only-r8-block03-pauli-group-order-20260502`
**Runner:** `scripts/pauli_group_order_check.py`
**Log:** `outputs/pauli_group_order_check_2026-05-02.txt`

## Cited authorities (one hop)

- [`AXIOM_FIRST_CL3_PER_SITE_UNIQUENESS_THEOREM_NOTE_2026-04-29.md`](AXIOM_FIRST_CL3_PER_SITE_UNIQUENESS_THEOREM_NOTE_2026-04-29.md)
  provides:
  - **(U2)** Per-site Hilbert H_x ≅ C² (Pauli rep) is the unique faithful
    2-dim Cl(3) representation, with γ_i ↦ σ_i.

This is the only load-bearing one-hop dependency.

## Admitted-context inputs

- **Pauli multiplication identity σ_a σ_b = δ_{ab} I + i ε_{abc} σ_c.**
  Standard identity, derivable from {σ_a, σ_b} = 2 δ_{ab} I and
  [σ_a, σ_b] = 2 i ε_{abc} σ_c which both follow from Cl(3) defining
  relations.
- **Group generation by closure under multiplication.** Standard finite-group
  construction.
- **Center, quotient, group order theorems.** Standard finite-group theory.

All three are pure mathematical / definitional facts; no admitted physics
conventions.

## Statement

Let σ_1, σ_2, σ_3 be the Pauli matrices acting on H_x ≅ C² (cited
per-site uniqueness). Define the **Pauli group**

```text
    P_1  :=  ⟨σ_1, σ_2, σ_3⟩  =  smallest multiplicative group containing all σ_i.
```

Then:

**(P1) Pauli multiplication identity.** For all a, b ∈ {1, 2, 3}:

```text
    σ_a σ_b  =  δ_{ab} I  +  i ε_{abc} σ_c                                 (1)
```

This forces phase factors ±1 and ±i to be in any closed multiplicative
extension.

**(P2) Order |P_1| = 16.** Concretely:

```text
    P_1  =  {±I, ±i I, ±σ_1, ±i σ_1, ±σ_2, ±i σ_2, ±σ_3, ±i σ_3}.        (2)
```

The 16 elements are: 4 phases (±1, ±i) × 4 distinct matrices (I, σ_1, σ_2, σ_3).

**(P3) Non-abelian.** P_1 is non-abelian: σ_1 σ_2 = i σ_3 ≠ -i σ_3 = σ_2 σ_1.

**(P4) Center.** Z(P_1) = {±I, ±i I} of order 4. Equivalently, the
center is generated by the scalar phase i I.

**(P5) Quotient is Klein four-group.** P_1 / Z(P_1) ≅ Z_2 × Z_2 (Klein
four-group). The four cosets are represented by {I, σ_1, σ_2, σ_3} mod
center.

**(P6) Element orders.** Every element g ∈ P_1 satisfies g^4 = I. The
orders of elements are: identity order 1; -I and σ_a have order 2; i I
and i σ_a have order 4.

## Proof

### Step 1 — Pauli multiplication table (P1)

By cited per-site uniqueness, σ_i are the standard Pauli matrices on C².
The Pauli identity σ_a σ_b = δ_{ab} I + i ε_{abc} σ_c follows from:
- {σ_a, σ_b} = 2 δ_{ab} I (anticommutator: Cl(3) defining relation)
- [σ_a, σ_b] = 2 i ε_{abc} σ_c (commutator: derived structure)

Adding: σ_a σ_b = (1/2) ({σ_a, σ_b} + [σ_a, σ_b]) = δ_{ab} I + i ε_{abc} σ_c.

Establishing (P1).

### Step 2 — Group order (P2)

Apply (P1) repeatedly to enumerate the multiplicative group ⟨σ_1, σ_2, σ_3⟩:
- Generators: σ_1, σ_2, σ_3.
- σ_a² = I (from a = b case of (1)). Forces I in the group.
- σ_a σ_b for a ≠ b = i ε_{abc} σ_c. Forces ±i σ_c.
- σ_b σ_a = -i ε_{abc} σ_c. Forces ∓i σ_c.
- Negation: σ_a (i σ_b) = i (σ_a σ_b) = -ε_{abc} σ_c.
- Powers of i: i², i³, i⁴ generate Z_4 = {1, i, -1, -i}.
- Combined: all elements of the form (±1, ±i) × (I, σ_1, σ_2, σ_3) appear.

Brute-force enumeration in the runner (BFS over multiplicative closure)
gives exactly 16 distinct matrices, matching (P2).

### Step 3 — Non-abelian (P3)

Direct: σ_1 σ_2 = i σ_3, and σ_2 σ_1 = -i σ_3. So [σ_1, σ_2] = 2 i σ_3 ≠ 0,
hence P_1 is non-abelian — establishing (P3).

### Step 4 — Center (P4)

An element c is central in P_1 if c g = g c for every generator g = σ_i.
By Test in the runner: only {±I, ±i I} commute with all three σ_i.

Algebraically: a candidate c = (±1, ±i) · σ_a satisfies c σ_b = σ_b c
iff (±1, ±i) · σ_a σ_b = (±1, ±i) · σ_b σ_a, i.e. σ_a σ_b = σ_b σ_a, which
fails for a ≠ b (Step 3). So no σ_a is central.

The remaining elements are scalars (±1, ±i) · I, all of which trivially
commute with everything. Hence Z(P_1) = {±I, ±i I} of order 4 —
establishing (P4).

### Step 5 — Quotient Klein 4 (P5)

|P_1 / Z(P_1)| = 16 / 4 = 4. The quotient is a group of order 4, hence
either Z_4 or Z_2 × Z_2.

Each σ_a satisfies σ_a² = I ∈ Z(P_1), so the image of σ_a in the quotient
has order 2 (squares to identity). A group of order 4 with all
non-identity elements of order 2 is the Klein four-group Z_2 × Z_2 —
establishing (P5).

### Step 6 — Element orders (P6)

For g = (phase) · M with phase ∈ {±1, ±i} and M ∈ {I, σ_a}:
- g² = (phase)² · M² = (phase)² · I.
- (phase)² ∈ {1, -1}, so g² ∈ {±I}.
- g⁴ = ((phase)²)² · I² = I.

Hence every g satisfies g⁴ = I — establishing (P6). ∎

## Hypothesis set used

- `axiom_first_cl3_per_site_uniqueness_theorem_note_2026-04-29`:
  provides per-site Pauli rep.
- Pauli multiplication identity (mathematical, derivable from Cl(3)
  defining relations).
- Group generation / center / quotient theorems (mathematical, admitted-context).

No fitted parameters. No observed values. No physics conventions admitted
beyond the cited per-site uniqueness theorem.

## Corollaries

C1. **Per-site Cl(3) projective rep of Z_2 × Z_2 over Z_4.** P_1 is a
Z_4 central extension of Klein four-group: 1 → Z_4 → P_1 → Z_2 × Z_2 → 1.
This is the canonical projective representation of qubit-level discrete
symmetry on a 2-dim Hilbert space.

C2. **Stabilizer formalism applies.** The framework's per-site Hilbert
admits the standard stabilizer formalism of quantum information theory,
since P_1 is exactly the single-qubit Pauli group used in stabilizer
codes. Stabilizer states are P_1-eigenvectors with eigenvalue +1.

C3. **Clifford group on per-site Hilbert.** The Clifford group C_1 of
order |C_1| = |U(2)| / |center extension| = 24 acts as P_1-normalizer
on H_x. Together {P_1, C_1} provide the standard discrete operator
hierarchy at the per-site level.

C4. **Multi-site Pauli group.** On the N-site Fock space, the multi-site
Pauli group P_N = ⟨σ_{i, x} : i = 1..3, x = 1..N⟩ has order |P_N| =
4 · 4^N (4 phases × 4 per-site choices for each of N sites). This is
the standard generalization to N qubits.

C5. **Discrete symmetry algebra exhausted at per-site level.** P_1 is the
maximal finite multiplicative subgroup of M_2(C) generated by σ_i (up to
center). Any further discrete symmetry of H_x is either central or
arises from a Clifford-circuit normalizer — not from new per-site
generators.

## Review classification

Positive theorem on the framework's per-site Pauli group order, derived
from a single cited one-hop dependency by elementary multiplicative
group enumeration in the Pauli basis. The runner exhibits the Pauli
multiplication table, brute-force enumeration of the 16 group elements,
non-abelianness, the central subgroup of order 4, the Klein 4 quotient,
and the order-divides-4 property — all at machine precision.
